questions.question: Approximate $\int_{0}^{4} \ln(x+1) \, dx$ using a left Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.
Use a right Riemann sum with $n = 4$ to estimate $\int_{0}^{2} 2^x \, dx$.

The table gives values of $h(x)$. Use a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to estimate $\int_{0}^{12} h(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 4 & 8 & 12 \\ \hline h(x) & 10 & 6 & 9 & 5 \end{array}$
Approximate the area under $f(x) = \sqrt{1 - x^2}$ on $[0,1]$ using a left Riemann sum with 5 subintervals.
The rate of oil leakage (L/min) is given by $r(t) = 10e^{-0.2t}$. Use a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to estimate the total leakage from $t = 0$ to $t = 6$.
questions.explanation: $\Delta t = 2$; right endpoints: $t = 2, 4, 6$. $r(2) \approx 6.703$, $r(4) \approx 4.493$, $r(6) \approx 3.012$. Sum $\approx 14.208$, times 2 = 28.416 L.

questions.question: For $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$ on $[1,5]$, use a midpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.

Given the table, use a left Riemann sum to approximate $\int_{0}^{8} f(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 2 & 3 & 5 & 7 & 8 \\ \hline f(x) & 2 & 5 & 4 & 3 & 6 & 1 \end{array}$
questions.explanation: Subintervals have unequal widths: [0,2], [2,3], [3,5], [5,7], [7,8]. Left endpoints: 0, 2, 3, 5, 7. Multiply by widths: $2\times2=4$, $5\times1=5$, $4\times2=8$, $3\times2=6$, $6\times1=6$. Sum = 29.

questions.question: Approximate $\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin^2 x \, dx$ using a right Riemann sum with $n = 4$.
A velocity function is $v(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t$. Use a left Riemann sum with 4 subintervals to estimate displacement from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.
Which of the following represents a midpoint Riemann sum with $n = 6$ to approximate $\int_{1}^{4} \sqrt{e^x} \, dx$?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered Riemann sum approximations.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! You understand the basics of Riemann sums. Review midpoint calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on identifying endpoints and calculating $\Delta x$ correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Review the definition of Riemann sums: $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$.
